items.q: Why can a profitable business still fail?
items.correct: Because cash flow and profit are different — a business may be profitable on paper but run out of cash to pay bills, especially if customers owe money or stock ties up funds.
items.misconception: If a business is profitable it cannot run out of money.
items.random_wrong: Profitable businesses fail only when employees steal.
items.why: Cash-flow problems sink profitable companies routinely. Profit is accounting; cash is liquidity. Students treat the two as equivalent and miss why businesses go bust despite paper profits.

items.q: What is a brand in marketing?
items.correct: A combination of name, design, symbols and associations that distinguishes a product and creates customer perception and loyalty — more than just a logo.
items.misconception: A brand is just the logo on a product.
items.random_wrong: A brand is the company that makes a product.
items.why: Branding is the sum of perceptions, not just visual identity. Coca-Cola the brand is much more than the script logo — it is associations, taste expectations, advertising history.

items.q: When can a laissez-faire leadership style be effective?
items.correct: When employees are highly skilled, self-motivated and need autonomy — common in creative or expert teams. It can fail with inexperienced staff or in crises requiring direction.
items.misconception: Laissez-faire leadership means the leader doesn't care about the business.
items.random_wrong: Laissez-faire leadership is illegal under employment law.
items.why: Laissez-faire is a deliberate choice to delegate, not negligence. Students moralise it as lazy management, missing its appropriateness for skilled autonomous workers and innovation-led settings.

items.q: Moving customer service to India is:
items.why: It's Offshoring. “Outsourcing only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is a QUALITATIVE location factor?
items.why: It's Skills available. “Land cost” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Steel plant freight: ore $20/t, distance 100km, rate $0.5/t-km. Total ($/t) =
items.why: The real figure is 70. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
